1: The Moabite oracle charges Moab with 'burning the bones of the king of Edom to lime' — a particularly egregious desecration of the dead. The burning of bones (saraf atzmot melek-Edom lasid) violated the ancient Near Eastern ethic that required proper burial even for enemies; the desecration of a king's remains denied him dignified existence in the afterlife under Moabite religious belief. This detail is notably a crime against Edom, not against Israel — showing that YHWH's moral jurisdiction extends beyond the covenant community to govern how nations treat each other.
2: Kerioth was a significant Moabite city, possibly identifiable with the modern site of Khirbet Qeiyafa or el-Qreyyat; the Mesha Stele (ca. 840 BCE), the most important Moabite inscription discovered, mentions YHWH in the context of Moabite-Israelite conflict. The 'tumult, shout, and sound of the ram's horn' (beshon beteru uvtru bequal shofar) describes the chaos of battle — the sonic landscape of military conquest in the ancient world. Moab's destruction 'in battle' is the fitting end for a nation that committed military atrocities.
3: The 'ruler from its midst' (shofet mikirba) and 'princes' (sarav) represent Moab's political elite — the administrative infrastructure that would need to be removed for Moab to cease functioning as an independent political entity. The removal of judges/rulers is consistent with ANE conquest strategy: without the administrative class, the nation cannot govern itself. Moab eventually became part of the Neo-Babylonian and later Persian administrative system, losing its independent political identity.
4: The Judah oracle is positioned just before the Israel oracle, structuring Amos's rhetorical trap: the northern kingdom audience (to whom Amos primarily speaks) would have cheered the judgment against their southern rivals as much as against the foreign nations. Judah's crime is covenantal rather than military: 'rejecting the law (Torah) of the LORD and not keeping his statutes.' This distinguishes the Judah oracle from the preceding foreign-nation oracles — Judah is judged for covenant violation, not for military atrocity. The 'false gods after which their ancestors walked' identifies the specific form of Judah's apostasy.
5: Jerusalem's 'fortresses' (armonot Yerushalayim) are the same term used for the foreign nations' citadels — YHWH's judgment falls on Jerusalem's defensive infrastructure with the same finality as it falls on Damascus or Tyre. This equivalence is shocking to a Judean audience: the city of David's palace-citadels are as vulnerable to YHWH's fire as the pagan capitals. The judgment of Jerusalem by fire anticipates the 586 BCE Babylonian burning, narrated in 2 Kings 25:9.
6: The Israel oracle, the climax of the nation-oracle series, indicts not military atrocities but economic injustice. 'Selling the righteous for silver and the needy for a pair of sandals' describes debt slavery: legally a person convicted of a debt could be sold into slavery (Leviticus 25:39-46), but Amos's charge is that the system was being abused — the 'righteous' (those with a legitimate case) were being sold anyway because judges were bribed. The specific mention of 'sandals' suggests trivially small debts being used as pretexts for slavery. This corresponds with the gap between rich and poor attested in 8th-century Israelite archaeological sites (large houses vs. small, ivory vs. pottery).
7: 'They trample the heads of the poor into the dust (hashafim al afar eretz berosh dallim)' — the idiom of 'putting one's head in the dust' is a Near Eastern expression for extreme subjugation and humiliation. 'Father and son go to the same young woman' — this is ambiguous: either the exploitation of female debt slaves (father and son sharing sexual access to a female bondservant, a violation of Leviticus 18:15) or the use of the same cult prostitute, defiling YHWH's holy name in either case. The phrase 'to profane my holy name' identifies this as the theological core of the indictment: social injustice is an act of blasphemy against YHWH.
8: 'They spread out pledged garments beside every altar' — Israelite law required that garments taken as collateral for loans be returned before nightfall (Exodus 22:26-27; Deuteronomy 24:12-13), especially cloaks of the poor who would need them to sleep. Spreading these garments out beside the altar as sleeping mats or couches shows these laws were openly flouted. 'In the house of their God they drink wine obtained by fines' — the courts imposed fines that were then consumed in feasts at the sanctuary: injustice literally being eaten and drunk in YHWH's name.
9: YHWH's recitation of his saving acts (vv9-11) grounds the covenant obligation in historical action. The Amorites whom YHWH destroyed 'before them' were the pre-Israelite inhabitants of Canaan — described in Numbers 13:28-33 as giants (the Anakim). The Deuteronomic conquest narratives (Josh 10-12) record the defeat of Amorite kings; Amos uses the tradition to contrast YHWH's actions on Israel's behalf with Israel's ingratitude. The 'height like the cedars and strength like the oaks' is conventional hyperbole for the overwhelming power of the pre-Israelite inhabitants.
10: 'I also brought you up from the land of Egypt, led you through the wilderness for forty years' — the Exodus and wilderness traditions are the foundational covenant events that establish YHWH's claim on Israel's obedience. Amos appeals to this history not as a comfort but as an accusation: the same YHWH who delivered them from Egypt and led them forty years in the wilderness is now bringing judgment because they have betrayed the relationship those events established. The forty years in the wilderness is a standard reference to the entire Sinai-to-Canaan period (Numbers 14:33; Deuteronomy 29:5).
11: 'I raised up prophets from among your sons and Nazirites from among your young men' — the raising of prophets and Nazirites is presented as a divine gift to Israel parallel to the conquest and exodus: YHWH gave them not just the land but the institutions (prophetic word, Nazirite consecration) necessary for covenant life. Nazirites took vows of separation (no wine, no haircut, no corpse contact — Numbers 6:1-21) as signs of special dedication to YHWH; they functioned as living symbols of covenant devotion. The rhetorical question 'Is this not so, O people of Israel?' (ha'ef ken benei Yisrael) demands acknowledgment of the evidence.
12: 'But you gave the Nazirites wine to drink and ordered the prophets: Do not prophesy.' The twin violations — forcing Nazirites to break their vows (destroying the symbol of covenant devotion) and silencing the prophets — represent the attempt to eliminate the two primary covenant-maintenance institutions. The command 'do not prophesy' (al tinavve'u) is the attempt to mute the divine voice itself. Amos himself faced this exact command from Amaziah (Amos 7:12-13: 'Go, flee to Judah... never prophesy again at Bethel'). The silencing of the prophets is self-referential — Amos is doing the very thing Israel is commanded not to let him do.
13: 'Watch — I will crush you in your tracks (aneq tachat tacham), as a cart loaded with sheaves presses down.' The agricultural image of the loaded threshing-cart pressing down on its axle is vivid: Israel's accumulated sin is the weight that is about to crush them under YHWH's judgment. The word aneq (I will crush/press) is used only here in the OT, possibly a technical term for the groaning of an overloaded cart. The image shifts from the earlier fire-and-exile imagery of the nation oracles to a more agricultural metaphor suited to the Israelite peasant audience.
14: The five-fold impossibility of escape in vv14-16 — the swift cannot flee, the strong finds no strength, the warrior cannot save himself, the bowman cannot stand his ground, the horse cannot deliver the rider — is a comprehensive demolition of military self-confidence. The specific list corresponds to the components of an Israelite (or any ANE) army: infantry (swift and strong), archers, cavalry. No military category is exempt from YHWH's judgment. This passage addresses the same pride of military strength that Amos condemns throughout the book (cf. Amos 6:13).
15: The continuation of the impossibility list: 'the bowman (tofes haqeshet) will not hold his ground; the swift-footed soldier will not escape; the horseman will not escape with his life.' The three elements — archer, light infantry, cavalry — represent the standard components of 8th-century BCE Near Eastern armies as confirmed in Assyrian reliefs and military records from the period. YHWH's judgment defeats the entire military apparatus simultaneously, leaving no category of soldier any advantage over another.
16: 'Even the most courageous warrior (amitz libo bagibborim; literally, the strong-hearted among the warriors) will flee away naked on that day.' The 'naked flight' (yanusu arom) of the most courageous indicates the complete dissolution of military cohesion: stripping off armor and weapons to run faster was the final act of a routed army. ANE military accounts frequently describe enemy soldiers throwing off their equipment in flight. 'On that day' links the prophecy to the Day of the LORD theology developed more fully in Amos 5.
